title: Git Cheat Sheet
description: 49 Git commands with flags, real-world use cases, and gotchas. Searchable and filterable, with a dedicated Recovery Commands section for undoing mistakes. Verified against official Git docs.

commands.title: Initialize a repository
commands.useCase: Start tracking a new project with Git in the current directory.

commands.title: Clone a repository
commands.useCase: Get a full local copy of a remote repository, including its entire history, to start working on it.

commands.title: Set your global commit name
commands.useCase: Set the name attached to every commit you make on this machine, before your first commit.

commands.title: Set your global commit email
commands.useCase: Set the email attached to every commit, which platforms like GitHub use to link commits to your profile.

commands.title: View all Git configuration
commands.useCase: Check which settings are active and where they came from, whether system, global, or local repo config, when something behaves unexpectedly.
commands.beginnerNote: Config is layered: system, then global, then local repo config, with later ones overriding earlier ones. A local .git/config override is a common reason a setting doesn't match what you expect from your global config.

commands.title: Stage changes
commands.useCase: Move a modified or new file into the staging area before committing it.

commands.title: Stage changes interactively by hunk
commands.useCase: Stage only specific changes within a file for a clean, atomic commit instead of staging everything in the file at once.

commands.title: Check working directory status
commands.useCase: See what's staged, unstaged, and untracked before committing. This is the command you should run before nearly every other Git command.

commands.title: Commit staged changes
commands.useCase: Save a snapshot of staged changes to the local repository history with a descriptive message.

commands.title: Amend the last commit
commands.useCase: Fix a typo in the last commit message or add a forgotten file to the previous commit instead of creating a new one.
commands.beginnerNote: Only amend commits that have NOT been pushed to a shared branch. Amending a pushed commit requires a force push, which rewrites history for everyone who already pulled it.

commands.title: View staged changes
commands.useCase: Review exactly what's about to be committed before running git commit, as a final sanity check.
commands.beginnerNote: Plain git diff only shows unstaged changes. If you've already run git add, you need --staged (or --cached) to see what will actually be committed.

commands.title: List branches

flags.description: List remote-tracking branches too, not just local ones

commands.useCase: See all local branches and which one is currently checked out.

commands.title: Create a branch
commands.useCase: Create a new branch pointing at the current commit, without switching to it yet.

commands.title: Create and switch to a new branch
commands.useCase: Start a new feature branch off your current commit in a single command.

commands.title: Switch to an existing branch

flags.description: Create the branch if it doesn't already exist, same as checkout -b

commands.useCase: Move to a different branch using the newer, more explicit command introduced to replace overloaded uses of checkout.

commands.title: Merge a branch
commands.useCase: Bring the changes from a feature branch into the branch you currently have checked out, such as merging a feature into main.
commands.beginnerNote: A merge that touches the same lines on both branches produces conflict markers you must resolve by hand before the merge can be completed.

commands.title: Rebase a branch
commands.useCase: Replay your branch's commits on top of the latest main to keep a linear, easy-to-read history before opening a pull request.
commands.beginnerNote: Never rebase a branch that other people are working on. Rebase rewrites commit hashes, and the history on shared branches diverges in ways that are painful to resolve.

commands.title: Apply a specific commit to the current branch
commands.useCase: Pull a single bugfix commit onto a release branch without merging the entire branch it originated on.
commands.beginnerNote: Cherry-pick creates a new commit with a different hash even if the content is identical. The original commit still exists on its original branch. This is a copy, not a move.

commands.title: List remotes
commands.useCase: Check which URL origin (or any other remote) actually points to before pushing, especially after cloning a fork.

commands.title: Add a remote
commands.useCase: Connect a local repository created with git init to a remote repository on GitHub, GitLab, or another host.

commands.title: Fetch remote changes
commands.useCase: Download new commits and branches from the remote without merging them into your current branch, to review before integrating.

commands.title: Fetch and merge remote changes
commands.useCase: Bring your local branch up to date with the remote in one step before starting new work.
commands.beginnerNote: git pull is git fetch plus git merge combined. If you'd rather review incoming changes first, or prefer rebasing over merging, run fetch and merge (or rebase) separately.

commands.title: Push local commits to a remote
commands.useCase: Upload your local commits to the shared remote branch so teammates and CI can see them.

commands.title: Force-push safely
commands.useCase: Push a rewritten history, such as after an interactive rebase, without blindly overwriting a teammate's work.
commands.beginnerNote: Safer than --force because it fails if someone else pushed to the branch since you last fetched. Use this instead of --force in almost every situation.

commands.title: Stash uncommitted changes
commands.command: git stash push -m "work in progress on login form"
commands.useCase: Temporarily shelve uncommitted work so you can switch branches to handle an urgent fix, then come back to it later.
commands.beginnerNote: Stash is a stack, not a single slot, so you can stash multiple times and pop them in order. Name your stashes with git stash push -m "message" or you will forget what each one contains.

commands.title: List stashes
commands.useCase: See every stash currently saved before deciding which one to restore.

commands.title: Restore the most recent stash
commands.useCase: Bring back your shelved work after switching back to the branch you were working on.
commands.beginnerNote: Pop can produce merge conflicts if the branch changed since you stashed. If you want to keep the stash in case the pop goes wrong, use git stash apply instead, which doesn't delete it from the stack.

commands.title: Delete a stash
commands.useCase: Remove a stash you no longer need, such as an outdated work-in-progress that's no longer relevant.
commands.beginnerNote: Dropped stashes are not immediately gone. They can often still be recovered with git fsck --unreachable for a short time before garbage collection, but don't rely on this.

commands.title: Revert a commit
commands.useCase: Undo a specific commit on a shared branch by creating a new commit that reverses it, safe even after the commit has been pushed.
commands.beginnerNote: Unlike reset, revert never rewrites history. It's the safe way to undo something on a branch other people have already pulled.

commands.title: Move HEAD back but keep changes staged
commands.useCase: Combine your last commit with new changes into a single commit, by uncommitting but keeping everything staged and ready to recommit.
commands.beginnerNote: Soft reset keeps changes staged, unlike a plain reset which unstages them. Useful specifically when you want to immediately recommit with an amended set of changes.

commands.title: Remove untracked files and directories

flags.description: Dry run, showing what would be deleted without actually deleting anything

commands.useCase: Wipe out build artifacts, generated files, and other untracked clutter to get back to a pristine working directory.
commands.beginnerNote: This permanently deletes untracked files with no undo. Always run git clean -n first to preview exactly what would be removed before adding -f.

commands.title: Stop tracking a file without deleting it
commands.useCase: Remove a file from Git tracking, such as one that should have been in .gitignore from the start, while leaving it on disk.
commands.beginnerNote: The file still exists locally after this. Only Git's tracking of it is removed. Add it to .gitignore too, or it will show up as untracked and be easy to accidentally re-add.

commands.title: Discard changes to a file (legacy syntax)
commands.useCase: Discard uncommitted changes to a single file, using the older syntax still seen in many existing scripts and tutorials.
commands.beginnerNote: This older overloaded syntax has been superseded by git restore, which is unambiguous about whether you're restoring a file or switching a branch. Prefer git restore in new scripts.

commands.title: View commit history
commands.useCase: See the commit history of the current branch, including author, date, and message for each commit.

commands.title: View a compact branching graph
commands.useCase: Visualize how branches diverged and merged across the whole repository in a single compact view.

commands.title: Show details of a specific commit
commands.useCase: Inspect the full diff and metadata of one specific commit without checking it out.

commands.title: Diff between two commits
commands.useCase: Compare what changed between two arbitrary points in history, such as two release tags.

commands.title: Show who last changed each line
commands.useCase: Find the commit that introduced a specific line, to understand the reasoning behind it before changing it.
commands.beginnerNote: The goal is to find context, not to blame people. Use it to understand why a line was written, not who wrote it, and check the linked commit message for the actual reasoning.

commands.title: List tags
commands.useCase: See all tags in the repository, such as version releases, before creating a new one.

commands.title: Create an annotated tag
commands.useCase: Mark a specific commit as an official release with a message, author, and date attached, unlike a lightweight tag.
commands.beginnerNote: Prefer annotated tags (-a) over lightweight tags for releases. They store the tagger, date, and message as a full Git object, which lightweight tags don't.

commands.title: Push tags to a remote
commands.useCase: Share a newly created release tag with the remote, since tags are not pushed automatically by a plain git push.
commands.beginnerNote: A regular git push does not push tags by default. Forgetting --tags after tagging a release is a common reason a CI pipeline watching for tags never triggers.

commands.title: Delete a local tag
commands.useCase: Remove a tag created by mistake, such as one pointing at the wrong commit, before anyone pulls it.
commands.beginnerNote: Deleting a local tag does not remove it from the remote. Also run git push origin :refs/tags/<tag-name> if it was already pushed.

commands.title: Undo the last commit but keep the changes
commands.useCase: You committed too early or bundled the wrong files, and want to fix the staging before recommitting, without losing any code.
commands.beginnerNote: Step by step: this moves the commit back to your staging area, so the commit itself disappears but your code changes are not lost. They show up as staged changes in git status. Fix what you need, then run git commit again.

commands.title: Undo the last commit and discard the changes
commands.useCase: You committed something completely wrong, like a debug commit with garbage content, and want it gone entirely, code included.
commands.beginnerNote: Step by step: this permanently destroys both the commit and all the code changes it introduced. There is no undo once this runs. Only use this if you are absolutely certain you do not want those changes back. If in doubt, use the non-destructive git reset HEAD~1 instead.

commands.title: Recover a deleted branch
commands.useCase: You or a teammate deleted a branch that still had unmerged work on it, and need it back.
commands.beginnerNote: Step by step: run git reflog to find the last commit hash the deleted branch was pointing at. Reflog shows every HEAD movement for 90 days by default, so the branch's last commit is still there until garbage collection runs. Then run git checkout -b <branch-name> <commit-hash> using that hash to recreate the branch pointing at exactly where it left off.

commands.title: Fix a wrong commit message that's already been pushed
commands.useCase: You pushed a commit with a typo or an incorrect message and need to correct it on the remote branch.
commands.beginnerNote: Step by step: first run git commit --amend to rewrite the message locally, then git push --force-with-lease to update the remote. This rewrites history, so anyone who already pulled the original commit will have a diverged branch. Coordinate with your team before doing this on a shared branch.

commands.title: Unstage an accidentally staged file
commands.useCase: You ran git add on the wrong file, or on everything with git add ., and want to remove just one file from staging before committing.
commands.beginnerNote: Step by step: this moves the file back to unstaged without touching its contents on disk. This is the correct modern command. Older tutorials show git reset HEAD <filename>, which also works, but restore is clearer about intent.

commands.title: Discard local changes in one file
commands.useCase: You made experimental edits to a file that didn't work out, and want to revert it back to the last committed version.
commands.beginnerNote: Step by step: this permanently discards all uncommitted changes to that file with no undo. Double-check with git diff <filename> before running this. Once it runs, the edits are gone for good.

commands.title: View the reflog to recover lost commits
commands.useCase: Recover commits that seem lost after a hard reset or an accidental branch deletion. Git keeps a local log of every HEAD movement for 90 days by default, so this is your undo button for things that feel undoable.
commands.beginnerNote: The reflog is local to your machine only. It is never pushed or shared, so it won't help recover another teammate's lost commits, only your own local history.
